coredata.dc:title: Multiplexed sandwich immunoassays using flow-injection electrochemiluminescence with designed substrate spatial-resolved technique for detection of tumor markers
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yan
Liu, Weiyan
Ge, Shenguang
Yan, Mei
Wang, Shaowei
Yu, Jinghua
Li, Nianqiang
Song, Xianrang
coredata.dc:description: Convenient sensor array for simultaneous multi-analyte testing was increasingly needed in clinical diagnosis. A novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor array for the sequential detection of multiple tumor markers was developed by site-selectively immobilizing multiple antigens on different electrodes. Disposable indium tin oxide (ITO) glass array was employed as detection platform. With a sandwich-type immunoassay format, the amount of carbon dots coated silica (SiO2@C-dots) labeled antibodies increased with the increment of antigens in the samples. The ECL signal from different immunosensors was collected in turn by a photomultiplier (PMT) with the aid of a home-made potential transformer equiped with a home-made multiplexed-switch. Using carcino embryonic antigen (CEA), prostate specific antigen (PSA) and α-fetoprotein (α-AFP) as model analytes, the proposed immunoassay exhibited excellent precision and sensitivity. For all three analytes, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for six times detection were lower than 7.1% and the detection limits were in the range of 0.003–0.006ngmL−1. The results for real sample analysis demonstrated that the newly constructed immunosensor array provided a rapid, simple, simultaneous multi-analyte immunoassay with high throughput, cost-effective and sufficiently low detection limits for clinical applications. Importantly, the novel individually addressable immunosensor array for multi-analyte immunoassay by introducing the ECL readout mechanism with the aid of the home-made potential transformer and multiplexed-switch could be a useful supplement to commercial assay methods in clinical chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multiplexed immunoassay
Electrochemiluminescence
Immunosensor array
Potential transformer
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the flow-injection ECL (FI-ECL) cell
Evaluation of cross-talk on immunosensor array
Performance of immunosensor array for three tumor markers
Possible mechanism for the ECL behavior of carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Research Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Research Foundation of China ( 21175058 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China ( ZR2012BZ002 , ZR2011BQ019 ) and Technology Development Plan of Shandong Province, China (Grant no. 2011GGB01153 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic drawings of the fabrication process of the ECL immunosensor.
Illustration of home-made multiplexed-switch and potential transformer with FI-ECL Cell.
Representative SEM image of silica nanospheres (A), TEM images of silica nanospheres (B), C-dots (C) and SiO2@C-dots (D). The inset of (C) was the HRTEM image of C-dots.

(A) EIS spectra of bare ITO (a), ITO/MPS (b), and ITO/MPS/CEA-Ab1 before (c) and after (d) blocking with BSA, ITO/MPS/CEA-Ab1/CEA (e), ITO/MPS/CEA-Ab1/CEA/C- dots@SiO2/CEA-Ab2 (f) in 0.1M KCl containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The frequency range was 0.01–100000Hz with the signal amplitude of 5mV. The inset showed the equivalent circuit to fit with obtained EIS spectra, where R S is the uncompensated resistance, Z w is the finite-length warburg element, C dl is the constant phase element attributed to the capacity of the double electric layer and R et is electron-transfer resistance attributed to the film modified on the electrode. (B) ECL intensity of four immunosensors on the array for the independent detection of CEA, PSA and α-AFP. WE1, WE2, WE3 and WE4 modified with CEA-Ab1, PSA-Ab1, α-AFP-Ab1 and BSA, respectively.

ECL signals of (A) CEA, (B) PSA and (C) α-AFP at the concentrations of 0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10 and 20ngmL−1, from a to i. Inset: corresponding calibration curves, where the error bars were the standard deviations for 5 times of parallel detection. All the tests are under the optimal conditions.
Recovery tests of CEA, PSA and α-AFP spiked in the human serum samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Human serum samples were 10-times diluted prior to assay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of six successive determinations.

head.ce:title: Multiplexed sandwich immunoassays using flow-injection electrochemiluminescence with designed substrate spatial-resolved technique for detection of tumor markers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Fluorine Chemistry and Chemical Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Information Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cancer Research Center Shandong Tumor Hospital, Jinan 250012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Convenient sensor array for simultaneous multi-analyte testing was increasingly needed in clinical diagnosis. A novel electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor array for the sequential detection of multiple tumor markers was developed by site-selectively immobilizing multiple antigens on different electrodes. Disposable indium tin oxide (ITO) glass array was employed as detection platform. With a sandwich-type immunoassay format, the amount of carbon dots coated silica (SiO2@C-dots) labeled antibodies increased with the increment of antigens in the samples. The ECL signal from different immunosensors was collected in turn by a photomultiplier (PMT) with the aid of a home-made potential transformer equiped with a home-made multiplexed-switch. Using carcino embryonic antigen (CEA), prostate specific antigen (PSA) and α-fetoprotein (α-AFP) as model analytes, the proposed immunoassay exhibited excellent precision and sensitivity. For all three analytes, the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for six times detection were lower than 7.1% and the detection limits were in the range of 0.003–0.006ngmL−1. The results for real sample analysis demonstrated that the newly constructed immunosensor array provided a rapid, simple, simultaneous multi-analyte immunoassay with high throughput, cost-effective and sufficiently low detection limits for clinical applications. Importantly, the novel individually addressable immunosensor array for multi-analyte immunoassay by introducing the ECL readout mechanism with the aid of the home-made potential transformer and multiplexed-switch could be a useful supplement to commercial assay methods in clinical chemistry.

► ECL biosensor array for multiplexed immunoassay based on spatial-resolved strategy was proposed. ► Carbon dots coated silicas were first prepared and used for signal amplification. ► Home-made potential transformer was used as the power supplier for detecting system. ► Disposable indium tin oxide glass array was employed as detection platform.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of tumor markers in blood or tissue has attracted increasing interest in the clinical cancer screening and early diagnostic applications (Jie et al., 2009; Dai et al., 2010). Among the common methods for cancer screening, immunosensor and immunoassay for single-tumor markers have been well developed (Wang et al., 2009; Jie et al., 2011). However, single marker immunoassay is often not sufficient for diagnosis purpose because most markers are not specific to a particular tumor and there are limitations on specificity and sensitivity. Panels of tumor markers can improve their diagnostic value (Zang et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2012). Therefore, the development of rapid, sensitive and selective simultaneous multi-analyte immunoassay with advantages of shortened analytical time, enhanced detection throughput and lowered detection cost (Barbee et al., 2010) has shown a growing demand for quantitative detection of multiple markers in clinical diagnosis and drug discovery (Lai et al., 2011).

Up to now, to achieve the simultaneous multi-analyte immunoassay in a single run, multi-label and the spatial-resolution strategies are the two dominant modes which have already been adopted in the reported literature (Hoheisel, 2006). For the former mode, multi-analyte immunoassay is obtained by utilizing various labels including enzymes (Elenis et al., 2007), fluorescent labels (Rissin and Walt, 2006) and quantum dots (Chen et al., 2009) to tag the antibodies or antigens of the corresponding analytes (one per analyte). However, the use of multiple labels reduces the convenience and increases the complexity. Combinations of multiple labels in a single run often require high discrimination between different analytes to avoid cross-talk, and often simultaneous detection of the signals from different labels inevitably involves a compromise in the optimal assay conditions, and the signal overlapping of different labels results in difficulty in accurate quantitation measurements. This strategy also encounters difficulty in finding more available labels, therefore, limits the number of analytes that can be detected in a single run.

Hence, an alternative spatial-resolution mode employing one universal label to detect all analytes on different spatial areas seems simpler and more suitable for high-throughput multi-analyte immunoassay than multi-label mode. At present, there are several commercially available automated or semiautomated analyzers for multiplexed protein measurements, employing fluorescence (Luminex, Myriad RBM), or ECL (Roche Diagnostics, Mesoscale Discovery) measurement technologies. Assay kits are available for measurement of up to 10 selected target proteins per sample (Rusling et al., 2010). These commercial instruments require specialized consumables, including sample well plates, chips, and reagent kits (Healy et al., 2007) which are expensive and, thus, limit their usefulness for point-of-care applications where resources are limited. Fluorescence-based detection strategies typically require laser sources and precise alignment of optical components, whereas electrochemical detection strategies offer robust, quantitative measurements using low-cost, simple instrumentation (Willner and Katz, 2003).

As is well-known, the ECL immunoassay, which combines the advantages of chemiluminescence and electrochemistry, not only shows high sensitivity and wide dynamic concentration response range but also is time- and spatial- controlled. In the ECL process, the luminescence signal is triggered by the EC reaction, which can be utilized to transduce the recognition event occurring on the electrode, and the ECL emitter is bound to the immunosensor surface by the immunoreaction. Thus, ECL immunoassay is believed to be well suited for the construction of sensor arrays as it is free from cross-talk. However, the bioanalysis based on those conventional luminescent reagents possesses some limitations. For instance, Ruthenium labeling at multiple sites may result in the loss of biological activity of the molecules (Fahnrich et al., 2001); luminol ECL signal is weak in neutral solution (Zhou et al., 2003); the release of Cd2+ can result in a cytotoxic effect and is a potential environmental hazard, which limits the application of quantum dots (QDs) (Derfus et al., 2004). As a result, it is urgent to search for environmental-friendly ECL reagents. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor QDs, photoluminescent C-dots are superior in chemical inertness, large two-photon excitation cross-sections, low cytotoxicity (Cao et al., 2007) and excellent biocompatibility (Baker and Baker, 2010). Furthermore, C-dots are highly promising due to their relatively high quantum yield and small size. Herein, C-dots were prepared easily and simply by electrooxidation of graphite in aqueous solution with low cytotoxicity and no photobleaching. The as-prepared C-dots had abundant carboxyl groups at their surfaces and exhibited strong ECL activity. To make it easy to be modified to attain amplifying signals, great efforts we have made toward the enhancement of detection sensitivity by using SiO2@C-dots as labels.

In the present study, a cross-talk-free immunosensor array by coupling an ECL readout mechanism for multiplexed immunoassay based on spatial-resolved strategy was proposed. To reduce the costs, a home-made potential transformer equiped with a home-made multiplexed-switch was used to replace the electrochemical workstation. Herein, a novel sandwich-type immunosensor array on ITO substrate using low-toxicity SiO2@C-dots as labels for signal amplification was first fabricated. Using CEA, PSA and α–AFP as model analytes, the proposed method exhibited good analytical performance. Such strategy shortened the immunoassay time and provided a promising platform for low-cost, rapid, and automated multiplexed immunoassay.

All reagents were of analytical-reagent grade and directly used for the following experiments without further purification. ITO (thickness of ITO layer: 150nm; resistance <15Ω/square; thickness of glass: 1.1mm) was obtained from Xiamen ITO Photoelectricity Industry (Xiamen, China). CEA, PSA, α-AFP standard solutions, the capture antibodies of them (CEA-Ab1, PSA-Ab1, α-AFP-Ab1) and the secondary antibodies of them (CEA-Ab2, PSA-Ab2, α-AFP-Ab2) were purchased from Shanghai Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 3-mercap- topropyl-trimethoxysilane (MPS), N-succinimidyl-4-(N-maleimido-methyl)- cyclohexane-1-carboxylate (SMCC), (3-aminopro-pyl)-trie-thoxy-silane (APTES) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Alfa Aesar China Ltd. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 10mM, pH 7.4) was used as washing buffer. PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1M K2S2O8 was used as the electrolyte in the measuring system. The ultra-pure water was obtained from a Lichun water purification system (≥18MΩ, Jinan, China). The clinical serum samples were provided by Shandong Tumor Hospital.

The ECL measurements were carried out on a self-built system including a home-made potential transformer as the power supplier which offered a potential for the electrolysis, and an IFFM-E chemiluminescence analyzer equipped with a PMT (Remex Electronic Institute Limited Co., Xi'an, China). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using a QUANTA FEG 250 thermal field emission SEM (FEI Co., USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-1400 microscope (Japan). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the prepared C-dots were recorded on an electronic microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20S5TWIN 200KV). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was studied using a CHI660B electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH instruments, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the flow-injection ECL (FI-ECL) cell
ce:section.ce:para: A simple and convenient method was developed to fabricate the four-electrode array on ITO glass. The procedure included designing the pattern for the electrodes, covering the surface of ITO glass with vaseline and etching ITO layer without vaseline coated by HCl (details in supporting information).

The FI-ECL cell (Fig. S1) was fabricated by a cuboid poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). Initially, the fabricated ITO slide glass was used as working electrodes (WEs). The FI-ECL cell mainly consisted of an ITO slide glass, two flow tubes (inlet and outlet) and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE). The WEs, two flow tubes, and RE were slided together by screws to form the airtight FI-ECL cell. The inlet and outlet were at the slide face of the cell and they were nearly at one line with ITO slide glass. At the bottom of the cell, a round optical window was made from which the ECL signal passed the ITO to the detection system was detected.

As a principal demonstration, a self-assembly integrated electrode array was utilized to perform tetradic-analyte immunoassay. As shown in Fig. S1C, the array was composed of four individually addressable ITO working electrodes. In addition, one Ag/AgCl reference electrode was set vertical. All working electrodes shared the same reference electrode.

The ITO was first dipped into piranha solution (98% H2SO4/30% H2O2, 7:3 in volume) for 12h. After washing thoroughly with ultra-pure water, the ITO was dried under nitrogen stream. The resultant substrates were then modified by being immersed into a toluene solution of MPS for 120min and then rinsed with toluene and followed by drying with N2.

The SMCC (1.5mgmL−1) and capture antibodies solution (20μgmL−1) were thoroughly mixed at the volume ratio of 1:1. After placed at 4°C for 0.5h, 5.0μL of this mixture was dropped onto the corresponding surface of the MPS-functionalized electrode array of ITO substrate and remained for 2h till the membrane was dried. And then, 50μL of 1.0% (w/v) BSA was dropped onto the antibody-immobilized array to block the unbound sites and remained for 60min. Finally, the array was rinsed thoroughly with the washing buffer and stored in 10mM PBS (pH 7.4) at 4°C unless use. Fig. 1 outlines the fabricating steps of the ECL immunosensor array.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, to carry out the competitive immunoassay of tumor markers, CEA, PSA, α–AFP and PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4), (10μL for each) was individually dropped onto the corresponding electrode of the array and dried at room temperature. After washing with PBS, 20μL of SiO2@C-dots labeled three different secondary antibodies (details in supporting information) were placed on the prepared three WEs, respectively. The as-prepared electrodes were air-dried at room temperature and the unbound SiO2@C-dots labeled secondary antibodies were washed out. After that, the modified immunosensors array was transferred to the home-made FI-ECL cell.

With the aid of the automatic multiplexed-switch, the prepared immunosensors were sequentially placed into the circuit to trigger the ECL reaction, with the excitation potential of (−1.3) V from a home-made potential transformer ( Fig. 2). The four ECL signals were successively collected by the PMT to perform near-simultaneously assay of CEA, PSA, α–AFP and BSA. All quantifying signals were calibrated by subtracting the ECL background response from the control sensor to avoid the effect from the nonspecific absorption.

The restriction of available labels is a bottleneck for increasing the number of analytes in multiplexed immunoassay (Fu et al., 2006). Therefore, a flow-through device with four WEs array composed of different capture antibodies was designed to improve the analytes throughput by combining with spatial-resolved technique (Fig. 1). Herein, SiO2@C-dots labeled antibodies were used for amplifying the ECL signals of their corresponding substrates. Different from common immunosensors (Tang et al., 2011; Cao et al., 2012), the ECL signals here was respectively detected by introducing the separate substrate zones on ITO, which increases the number of analytes. To reduce the expense of the device, a home-made potential transformer (Fig. 2B, $ 3) was used instead of the common used electrochemical workstation ($ 8300). And spatial-resolved immunoassay was realized by a home-made multiplexed-switch (Fig. 2A, $ 2) to change the circuits when the ECL signal from one WE was collected. There are four WEs on ITO, indicating the zone-resolution is enhanced and at least four analytes could be detected in a single run with a single PMT.

To reduce the interference from surroundings, the home-made potential transformer composed of two power supply systems: detecting circuit supplied by lithium ion battery (3.0V) and the power of operating system from the computer USB port. The operating interface contains display window and setup buttons. The display window can show export channel (3rd digit) and its corresponding running time (1st two digits) simultaneously. The potential transformer can only display the voltage (+0.01) - (+3.00) V operated by the setup buttons. Certainly, (−3.00) - (−0.01) V can also be operated on the working system when reverse the positive to negative. The operating interface of the home-made multiplexed-switch is almost the same as that of home-made transformer with the difference on that it has eight channels (CHs) to be exported. In this paper, WE1–WE4 were connected to CH2, CH4, CH6 and CH8, respectively. Their working time was 20s with 30s intervals in turn.

Since SiO2 nanoparticles were employed as the carriers for C-dots immobilization, monodispersed SiO2 nanoparticles with similar surface morphologies were vital for consistent loading of the same amount of C-dots on each microsphere. This in turn would influence the loading of the secondary antibodies and the sensitivity, reproducibility and analytical performance of the resulting immunosensor. In this work, the prepared SiO2 nanoparticles display a chemically clean and structure homogenized microsphere. The diameter of the SiO2 nanoparticles was ∼110nm, which was demonstrated with SEM and TEM images (as shown in Fig. 3A and B).

As shown in Fig. 3C, TEM image suggested that the size of the C-dots had a uniform spherical shape and was narrowly distributed with diameters in the range of 4–5nm. The inset was a HRTEM image of the fluorescent fractions, revealing clearly that C-dots were monodisperse. Coating of the C-dots onto the surface of SiO2 nanoparticles was achieved through acylamide binding in the presence of EDC as the activator. Briefly, APTES was first coupled to the hydroxyl group on the surface of silica nanospheres to yield an amino-terminated self-assembled monolayer. Subsequently, the carboxylic groups located on the surface of C-dots (Zheng et al., 2009) were reacted with amino distal points to form SiO2@C-dots. Coating of C-dots on silica nanospheres was demonstrated by UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra, as well as the color change under sunlight and UV illumination (λ ex=365nm) (the inset in Fig. S2) (details in supporting information). The coating of C-dots on silica nanospheres was also confirmed by TEM image (as shown in Fig. 3D). After the coating process, C-dots were deposited on the surface of silica nanospheres, probably in a uniform fashion. And the diameter of SiO2@C-dots was ∼130nm, which indicated the thickness of C-dots layer was about 10nm. All these results confirmed that C-dots had been successfully planted onto the surface of silica nanospheres with excellent dispersivity. Such good dispersivity prevented the particles from agglomeration, a common problem that occurs when small nanoparticles as biological labels used.

The surface morphology of an immunosensor is a vital factor affecting the performance of the sensor. Therefore, the morphologies of bare and MPS modified ITO WE were characterized by SEM (see in supporting information).

Previous studies have revealed that the detailed information about the immunosensor preparation and the immuno-binding on the immunosensor could be investigated by EIS (Nassef et al., 2009). As shown in curve a ( Fig. 4A), the bare ITO exhibited a small semicircle which was characteristic of infinitesimal electric resistance. All modified electrodes showed a large increase in diameter over that of the bare ITO electrode, which indicated much higher R et values after modification. After MPS membrane was modified on ITO, a remarkable increase in the R et value was observed (curve b). The reason may be that the formed MPS membrane stopped [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− from approaching the surface of electrode. When capture antibodies were modified onto the electrode, the curve rose notably (curve c). Curve d denoted that the ITO was successively blocked by BSA. The result displayed the similar increase of the resistance, which was consistent with the fact that the hydrophobic layer of the protein insulated the conductive support and perturbed the interfacial electron transfer. Further binding of antigens retarded the interfacial electron transfer of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− pair, which provided evidence of the successful formation of the immunocomplex (curve e). It has been reported that the antigen–antibody complex could act as an inert block layer. It could inhibit ion diffusion and change electrical capacitance, which both intensively affected the electrochemical impedance of electrodes during the formation of antigen–antibody complex (Su et al., 2011). This phenomenon is consistent with the observed greatly increased R et value via the anchor of Ab2. In the final step, the attachment of {SiO2@C-dots/Ab2} bioconjugates gave rise to an additional barrier towards the access of the redox probe to the electrode, which resulted in a further increment in the electrode-transfer resistance (curve f). All the results suggested that the immobilized CEA on the electrode could retain their native bioactivity and preliminarily apply for the determination of CEA. And these increases can entirely demonstrate the successful assembly processes on the immunosensor array. In addition, an equivalent circuit was utilized to fit the EIS (details in supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cross-talk on immunosensor array
ce:section.ce:para: It is speculated that the precise localization of immobilizing antigen/antibody on the individual surfaces of the multi-electrode array must be performed and the recognition for different targets must be only from different immobilized capture antibodies on each sensing electrode for multiple analysis. Therefore, the ECL cross-talk among four immunosensors on the array fabricated was examined. Fig. 4B showed the ECL intensity of four electrodes on the immunosensors array for the independent detection of CEA, PSA, α–AFP or BSA. It can be seen that the ECL intensity (3661a.u.) at WE1 was much higher than that at WE2 (315a.u.), WE3 (342a.u.) and WE4 (148a.u.) for the detection of 0.1ngmL−1CEA (Fig. 4B-a). When the CEA concentration increased from 0.1ngmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1, the ECL intensity increased from 3661a.u. to 5852a.u. at WE1 while no obvious signal changes were observed at WE2-WE4 electrodes (Fig. 4B-b). This indicated that there was no obvious cross-talk among the examined electrodes. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the average ECL intensity obtained at any one of the electrodes for tetradic measurements were less than 5%. Furthermore, the same phenomenon was observed for the independent detection of PSA and α–AFP. As shown in Fig. 4B-c and d, it can be seen that the ECL intensity at WE2 (3712a.u.) was much higher than that at WE1 (325a.u.), WE3 (347a.u.) and WE4 (168a.u.) for the detection of 0.1ngmL−1 PSA (Fig. 4B-c). When the PSA concentration added from 0.1 to 0.5ngmL−1, the ECL intensity went up from 3712a.u. to 5913a.u. at WE2 with no obvious signal changes observed at WE1, WE3 and WE4 (Fig. 4B-d). These results also evidently supported that the ECL cross-talk among four electrodes was negligible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of immunosensor array for three tumor markers
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions (the optimization process was in supporting information), the analytical performance of the immunosensor array was examined. The results showed that the response ECL intensity increased with the concentration increment of CEA, PSA and α–AFP and the ECL intensity was directly proportional to the concentration logarithm in the range from 0.01 to 50ngmL−1 (as shown in Fig. 5). The detection limits for CEA, PSA and α-AFP at a signal deducted three times of noise were estimated to be 0.006, 0.003 and 0.005ngmL−1, respectively, which were lower than that of previously reported (Wilson et al., 2005; Wu et al., 2011). Since the cutoff values of the three tumor markers in the diagnostic tests were 3ngmL−1 for CEA, 4ngmL−1 for PSA and 25ngmL−1 for α–AFP, the sensitivity of the proposed method was satisfied for practical applications (Liu et al., 2009; Reissigl et al., 1995; Yang et al., 2009).

As the reproducibility is a very important feature for immunosensor, it is necessary to investigate the reproducibility of the developed immunosensor array. It was evaluated with intra- and inter-assay precision. Intra-assay precision of the immunosensor array prepared at the same batch was carried out by assessment of two concentration levels (0.50ngmL−1 and 1.00ngmL−1) for six immunosensor arrays. Inter-assay precision was estimated by determining two levels of antigens with six immunosensor array made at different batches. Both the intra- and the inter-assay RSDs were in the range of 4.5–9.6% (Table S1). These results validated an acceptable reproducibility of the immunosensor array which could be ascribed to the simplicity of the label and the immunosensor array preparation.

The stability of immunosensor array fabricated was also examined. The immunosensor array stored at 4°C for 25 days showed that the steady-state ECL intensity for the detection of 0.50ngmL−1 CEA, 0.50ngmL−1 PSA and 0.50ngmL−1 α-AFP was 87.6%, 94.2% and 91.3% of the initial steady-state ECL intensity, respectively. Consequently, the proposed immunoassay system possessed perfect stabilization in practical applications.

To evaluate the potential application of this multiple immunosensor array, serum samples obtained from Shandong Cancer Hospital were assayed by the proposed method in this work. Before the assay, the serum samples must be diluted appropriately to ensure that antigen concentrations were in the linear range. The CEA levels in the three human sera were determined to be 1.7, 0.6 and 2.9ngmL−1, which were in acceptable agreement with the reference values (Lin, 1980). Known amounts of CEA, PSA and α–AFP standard solution were added into the diluted samples to perform the recovery tests. The results listed in Table 1 showed satisfied recoveries of 93–102%, 90–98%, and 96–105% for CEA, PSA and α–AFP, respectively. The RSDs for the three analytes were all less than 7.1%, indicating the acceptable accuracy. Based on the above results, the multiple immunosensor array fabricated in this work could be potentially applied in clinic tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the ECL behavior of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
From the ECL mechanism for CdS and CdTe QDs (Zhang et al., 2012; Jie et al., 2011), the ECL mechanism of C-dots system could be inferred. The SiO2@C-dots immobilized on the ITO through immunoreaction were reduced to nanocrystalline species (C-dots−·), while the coreactant S2O8 2_ was reduced to the strong oxidant SO4 −. Then SO4 −··could react with the negative charged C-dots−· through electron transfer, which produced the excited state (C-dots*) to emit light. The possible ECL mechanisms could be inferred as the following Eqs. (1)–(4):
(1)
C - dots +
e
−
→
C - dots
−
(2)
S
2
O
8
2 −
+
e
−
→
SO
4
2 −
+
SO
4
−
(3)
C - dots
−
+
SO
4
−
→ C - dots ⁎ +
SO
4
2 −
(4)
C -
dots ⁎
→ C - dots + h v

In summary, a novel individually addressable immunosensor array for multi-analytes immunoassay by combining the ECL readout mechanism with the home-made multiplexed-switch and potential transformer was successfully designed for the first time. The immunosensor fabricated in the array realized the selective immobilization of different antibodies at desired positions on a single array. Also, the immunoassay in sandwich-type performed by site-selectively immobilizing multiple antigens on different electrodes simplified the operational process of multi-analyte analysis. Such immunosensor array fabricated had attractive advantages such as high sensitivity, simple operation, negligible cross-talk, fine reproducibility, good storage stability and successful application to determine CEA, PSA and α-AFP concentrations in serum samples. As a result, this work provided a promising, addressable and simple strategy for the multi-detection of tumor markers. Moreover, the throughput of the assay could be further enhanced by integrating more micro-electrodes to construct a high-density micro-array, which could facilitate the application of multi-analytes analysis in point of care testing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Research Foundation of China (21175058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002, ZR2011BQ019) and Technology Development Plan of Shandong Province, China (Grant no. 2011GGB01153).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2012.09.044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the American Chemical Society
Journal of the American Chemical Society
